caption: Annotation-difficulty examples for Sentinel-2 iceberg chips. The panels contrast the source image, preliminary annotation, cleaned binary target, and the main annotation decision introduced by the v4_clean workflow.

Dataset construction workflow. Two annotation sources merged through three deterministic cleaning operations to produce the binary v4_clean train / validation / test splits. Sources: Roboflow polygons (single-class iceberg, SAM3 smart-select + manual review, SZA >= 65) and Fisser (2024) annotation arrays (3-class ocean / iceberg / shadow numpy arrays distributed as Python pickle files, SZA < 65). Cleaning in order: (1) shadow merge - Fisser shadow pixels reclassified as iceberg so all chips share a binary target; (2) 40 m component filter - connected components below 16 px (40 m root length at the 10 m S2 GSD) dropped from both sources; (3) annotation-aware IC pixel mask - bright non-annotated pixels in training chips with IC fraction >= 15% zeroed prior to training. Validation and test chips are never masked. Output: stratified 65 / 15 / 25 split = 551 / 137 / 228 chips, test capped at 57 per SZA bin.

Base segmentation workflow schematic. Two methods operate directly on B08 reflectance, while learned methods share a UNet++ probability map before direct segmentation, fixed-threshold binarization, Otsu binarization, or CRF refinement.

Per-iceberg evaluation workflow. Ground-truth and predicted components are matched by Hungarian assignment on 1 - IoU, unmatched components are counted as false negatives or false positives, and matched pairs feed MAE, IoU, and MSE summaries.

Experimental progression visual. Phase A walks dataset cleaning and balancing choices with one reader-facing motivation per step; Phase B freezes the selected dataset and compares base segmentation workflows, with top-hat variants treated as a separate downstream post-processing result.
Per-pair root-length MAE by SZA bin for the method comparison. Lower values indicate smaller matched-iceberg size error, making this the most direct Fisser-comparable accuracy view.

Predicted versus reference iceberg area, per SZA bin, all six methods overlaid with cool tones for non-learning (TR, OT) and warm tones for learning (UNet, UNet_TR, UNet_OT, UNet_CRF). Solid black 1:1 line and shaded +-25 percent over- and under-estimation band. Inset table reports per-method n_matched / n_gt (= match rate) and MAE on iceberg root length (m) cross-referencing Table 1 / Table 3 / Fig. 6.

Per-pair bias (top) and Delta-Bias vs TR baseline (bottom) by reference area bin, one panel per SZA bin (mirroring Fig. 7 and Fig. 9). Sharey within row so cross-SZA comparison is on common scale. P10-P90 band shaded on bias row. Color palette matches Fig. 7 (UNet_TR pink). Area bin edges match Fisser (2025) Fig. 16.

Per-pair relative error in iceberg area as a function of reference area bin, one panel per SZA bin (mirroring Fig. 7) and one line per method. Faint shaded band shows the P10-P90 spread per method per bin; mean is the solid line. Method palette matches Fig. 7 (UNet_TR pink).

Per-SZA-bin chip examples with reference (cyan dashed) and UNet_CRF predicted (bright purple solid; matches UNet_CRF in Figs. 7-9) iceberg outlines, picked at three per-pair RE positions: worst-positive, near-zero, worst-negative. Each panel is cropped with a 20 px pad around the matched-pair iceberg bbox so outlines have breathing room and are not clipped. A 10 m scale bar (1 Sentinel-2 pixel) is drawn at the bottom-left of each panel. Only the highlighted iceberg is the example.

Phase A lt65 dataset-progression leaderboard from results.md. A0 is the best validation-IoU configuration, while later cleaning and balancing variants underperform on this split. Source: paper-writing/results.md.
Phase A 2x2 from results.md. Preprocessing dominates the lt65 validation-IoU change, while null-chip injection has a smaller effect. Source: paper-writing/results.md.

Probability calibration audit from results.md. A2 shifts most pixels into the 0.20-0.35 probability range, explaining the failure of fixed-threshold post-processing on that run. Source: paper-writing/results.md.
Base six-method root-length MAE table from results.md. UNet_CRF has the lowest MAE in three of four SZA bins; UNet_OT wins the lt65 bin. Source: paper-writing/results.md.
Base six-method matched-pair IoU table from results.md. UNet_CRF again leads in three of four SZA bins, with UNet_OT leading lt65. Source: paper-writing/results.md.
Base six-method detection table from results.md. Match rate and precision disclose selection bias before comparing MAE across methods. Source: paper-writing/results.md.

Top-hat post-processing root-length MAE table from Overleaf Table 9. These +TH variants are the separate Segmentation -> top-hat -> segmentation++ sensitivity path. Source: paper-writing/overleaf/2026-04-17_template-plan-md/main.tex.
Top-hat post-processing IoU table from Overleaf Table 10. It keeps the +TH sensitivity branch separate from the six base methods. Source: paper-writing/overleaf/2026-04-17_template-plan-md/main.tex.

Top-hat post-processing detection table from Overleaf Table 11. The +TH branch trades precision for additional recovered candidate matches. Source: paper-writing/overleaf/2026-04-17_template-plan-md/main.tex.

needed_edits: Full HPC run over 23981 chips (all regions, all SZA bins). At production cutoff 0.15, 41% of chips would be skipped overall; per (region, SZA bin) skip-rate ranges from 27% (KQ sza_70_75) to 79% (SK sza_70_75). Sea-ice exposure dominates the gap, not SZA per se.
caption: Q1 empirical answer: per-chip ic_frac = mean(B08 >= 0.22) ECDF over 23981 chips, with per-(region, SZA bin) skip-rate bars at cutoffs in {10, 15, 20, 25, 30}%. Source: iceberg-rework/scripts/script_check_answers/q01_ic_cutoff_sweep.py.

needed_edits: Full HPC run over 23981 chips. Production floor 0.10 skips 6%; raising to 0.15 jumps to 41%; offset-corrected equivalent 0.20 skips 51%. The histogram has a sharp spike just above 0.10 (open-water chips Otsu picks up just past the noise floor) and a heavy tail past 0.5 (sea-ice / land-edge chips).
caption: Q7 empirical answer: per-chip Otsu threshold on B08 distribution over 23981 chips, with floor candidates {0.10, 0.15, 0.20} marked. Source: iceberg-rework/scripts/script_check_answers/q07_otsu_floor_distribution.py.

needed_edits: Ran on v4_clean VAL split (all 137 chips matched against Y_validation.pkl, 0 skipped) using the production baseline_v3_balanced_aug checkpoint. Pixel-level F1 climbs monotonically from 0.400 at tau=0.05 to 0.486 at argmax tau=0.95. Production tau=0.22 (nearest grid point 0.20) gives F1=0.4305; delta to argmax is +0.055. Recall stays above 0.77 across the range; precision is the binding constraint and the curve has not plateaued by tau=0.95, so the 0.05-step grid likely under-locates the true F1 maximum and reusing 0.22 underweights precision on this calibration.
caption: Q15 empirical answer: pixel-level F1, IoU, precision, recall vs probability threshold tau on the v4_clean val split (n=137). Source: iceberg-rework/scripts/script_check_answers/q15_unet_threshold_f1.py.

needed_edits: Ran on 171 test chips (same overlap set as Q15). 0% would-skip rate at every candidate cutoff in {0.005, 0.01, 0.02, 0.05}: every chip's range_p exceeds the production 0.01 floor by a wide margin. The 0.01 floor is comfortably non-binding on this population.
caption: Q16 empirical answer: per-chip range_p = max - min P(iceberg) histogram over 171 test chips, with skip-rate bars at flat-prob cutoffs in {0.005, 0.01, 0.02, 0.05}. Source: iceberg-rework/scripts/script_check_answers/q16_flat_prob_distribution.py.

needed_edits: Ran on 171 test chips. 52% of chips have Otsu < 0.3; 100% have Otsu < 0.5. A 0.5 floor would clip every chip and remove 22.5% of iceberg pixels overall; a 0.3 floor would clip 52% and remove 19%. The model is generally confident on iceberg pixels but per-chip Otsu sits below 0.5 for the entire population, so a 0.5 floor is too aggressive.
caption: Q17 empirical answer: per-chip Otsu(P(iceberg)) histogram over 171 test chips with candidate floors {0.3, 0.5, 0.7}, plus per-(region, SZA bin) iceberg-pixel reduction under the 0.5 floor. Source: iceberg-rework/scripts/script_check_answers/q17_otsu_on_prob_floor.py.

needed_edits: Full HPC run: 14109 of 23981 chips passed the IC filter. 30.5% of pre-filter polygons are <= 1 pixel and 46.7% are <= 2 pixels. The production 100 m^2 cutoff (>= 1 px) keeps every polygon, so it is not a salt-and-pepper filter. A 1-iteration binary opening before vectorisation would drop 71.9% of kept polygons (476187 -> 133568) and 13.6% of total area (1078.95 -> 931.76 km^2).
caption: Q2 empirical answer: TR polygon-area distribution pre 100 m^2 cutoff over 14109 IC-passing chips, with the kept-polygon counts compared against a 1-iteration binary-opening variant per (region, SZA bin). Source: iceberg-rework/scripts/script_check_answers/q02_polygonisation_artifacts.py.
